text: When they are punished, they do not respond with submission or cooperative “appeasement.” Being punished does not reliably lead them to increase their contributions in subsequent decisions. Instead, they often continue defecting and, in many cases, increase or maintain high levels of punishment against others afterward. text: When punished, this player generally does not respond with all-out vendetta behavior. They accept being deducted in multiple episodes without switching into a purely spiteful strategy. However, there are indications that recent punishment influences both their contributions and their own use of punishment. After receiving deductions, they sometimes shift toward free-riding, suggesting that being punished does not reliably bring them into line with cooperative norms. Instead, they sometimes appear to interpret punishment as part of a hostile environment and respond by protecting their own payoff rather than trying harder to be “good.” In other episodes, after being punished while they were cooperating, they still continue to contribute in later rounds, implying some tolerance and willingness to re-engage with cooperation rather than permanent disengagement. There are a few instances consistent with retaliatory punishment: they increase punishment shortly after being on the receiving end and target players who are associated with aggressive or misaligned actions. Still, this retaliation is restrained; they do not commit large resources to punishing any single punisher repeatedly. Their stance can be summarized as “resentful but pragmatic”: they neither fully forgive nor fully escalate. They adjust behavior and occasionally strike back, but they remain cost-aware and avoid self-destruction.

text: When punished, this player remains behaviorally steadfast and continues both full cooperation and active punishment. They do not respond to being sanctioned by reducing their contribution, suggesting they do not interpret punishment as a signal to free-ride less (they are already fully cooperative) nor as a reason to disengage. Instead, they maintain their existing norms and strategy, indicating resilience and a strong internal rule about “how one ought to play,” largely independent of others’ attempts to shape their behavior. Their reaction to being punished is not clearly retaliatory toward specific punishers, though they have both the information and the tools to retaliate. Given their consistent use of punishment and the presence of identifiable punishers, it is possible they incorporate some revenge motives, but this cannot be confirmed from the data and remains low-confidence. What is observable is that punishment against them does not seem to make them more compliant in the sense of becoming less punitive or more self-protective; instead, they continue to enforce norms at high cost. They display low forgiveness in the sense that they do not relax their punitive stance toward non-cooperators over time, but whether they specifically target past punishers is unknown.

cluster_intro: This group of players demonstrates a remarkable resilience in the face of punishment, maintaining high levels of cooperation despite experiencing sanctions. Rather than retaliating or reducing their contributions, they often respond with increased cooperation or remain steadfast in their contributions. Their behavior suggests a commitment to group norms and a focus on collective well-being over individual grievances.

text: This player is punished by at least one other participant yet shows no observable shift in either contribution or punishment behavior thereafter. They neither reduce their own cooperation nor cease punishing others after being targeted, indicating that they do not respond with visible retaliation or withdrawal of cooperation. Because punishers are identifiable, the lack of observable change suggests either indifference to the sanction or a deliberate decision to stay on their prior course despite it. There is no evidence that they try to appease punishers—cooperation is already maximal and remains so—nor evidence of counter-punishment, though limited information on their exact punishment targets leaves retaliation against the specific punisher as unknown. Overall, their pattern is consistent with a principled, rule-driven stance: they continue fully contributing and enforcing norms regardless of having been punished, showing behavioral resilience and a low degree of reactive emotionality that would manifest as immediate behavioral shifts.

text: When they are punished, the player does not retaliate and does not alter their cooperative behavior. They experience a nontrivial deduction early on, at a time when they themselves are fully cooperative, yet they neither reduce their own contribution nor visibly redirect punishment toward likely suspects as revenge. Instead, they maintain full contribution and continue to focus punitive efforts on clear free-riders. This indicates a non-retaliatory, norm-focused orientation: being punished does not shift their target set away from low contributors toward personal enemies. Because punishers are identifiable in principle, their failure to retaliate suggests either that they do not prioritize personal retribution, that they discount the informational value of that one punishment event, or that they accept being punished as a possible side-effect of a noisy environment. There is no evidence that they “reward” compliance through reduced punishment beyond simply ceasing to punish once their contribution norm is broadly met. They also do not display signs of strategic appeasement; they do not change behavior to avoid further punishment, but rather hold their cooperative course and continue enforcing norms. Their response profile is thus forgiving in the practical sense (no counter-punishment, no contribution drop) and strongly committed to their own standards, even when they are themselves treated unfairly.

Because punisher identities are observable to the punished in this environment, the player has the information needed to directly retaliate. text: When this player is punished, they do not clearly adjust their contribution behavior in a simple compliance way. text: They are repeatedly punished, sometimes quite heavily, but never respond with direct retaliation (no punishment costs targeted back at frequent punishers) and never respond by sustaining high contributions as a “compliance” reaction. Their contribution behavior following punishment is mixed: at times they move from contributing to not contributing despite being punished, and at other times they contribute after having been punished earlier, but these shifts do not show a clear pattern of “I got punished, therefore I must cooperate.” Instead, they maintain a largely selfish contribution strategy and simply absorb punishment as a cost of free-riding. Overall, they display tolerance of being targeted: they neither escalate conflict nor clearly yield to it. This looks like a player who expects punishment as part of the environment and treats it as background noise rather than as a message to which behavior must be adapted, consistent with a payoff-focused or emotionally detached style.

Rather than engaging in precise one‑for‑one revenge, they often broaden their sanctioning to additional players in the same period, which points to a generalized anger or “global punishment” response when they feel mistreated. There is no evidence of a compliant reaction (i.e., permanently increasing their own contributions after being punished); their contributions remain volatile and often low even after strong sanctions. They neither “forgive and move on” nor clearly learn to avoid punishment by conforming more; instead they accept being a frequent punishment recipient and respond by punishing others intensely.

text: The player is punished multiple times across the game, sometimes heavily. Their behavioral response to being punished is not straightforwardly compliant or retaliatory. In social‑psychological terms, they appear relatively thick‑skinned toward punishment: they neither consistently bow to it nor engage in vendettas. text: This player is repeatedly punished by others, often quite heavily, at various times in the game. Their response is not simple compliance or retaliation. Their punishment decisions do not systematically line up with those who targeted them, and they often abstain from punishment right after receiving substantial deductions. In aggregate, their response to punishment is best described as “partially indifferent and idiosyncratic.” They sometimes adjust behavior in more cooperative directions after periods of being sanctioned, but not reliably enough to infer that they treat punishment as a clear signal to conform. They appear to accept punishment as a cost of their opportunistic play rather than as a binding constraint.

text: The player is punished many times by multiple group members and often quite harshly, yet they do not respond with symmetrical retaliation against those identifiable punishers. They never initiate a punishment campaign against their main punishers, despite having full information about who is targeting them. This suggests low vengefulness and a relatively high tolerance for being sanctioned. Their contributions do not reliably increase after being punished; they continue to free-ride in many subsequent rounds. At the same time, their occasional bursts of full cooperation appear after or amidst repeated punishment episodes, but since they are not directed specifically in response to a single punisher, these look more like attempts to test whether becoming cooperative will soften the general environment rather than compliance toward a particular enforcer. They also do not shift their reward or punishment allocation toward or away from those who punish them; there is no clear attempt to court favor with punishers, nor to avoid them. This pattern points to a player who is relatively unmoved by punitive threats in terms of direct reciprocity: they neither systematically comply with nor systematically retaliate against punishers. Their focus remains on their own payoff strategy and on a small set of normative concerns about the most extreme deviants, not on tit-for-tat with individual punishers.

cluster_title: Responses to Punishment in Cooperative Settings
cluster_intro: This cluster highlights players who experience punishment without clear patterns of retaliation or compliance. Their responses to being punished are varied and context-dependent, often resulting in mixed contributions rather than a straightforward shift toward cooperation or defection. text: When the player is punished, including in cases where they suffer very large deductions and can identify likely punishers, they do not retaliate via counter-punishment. Nor do they clearly comply with the implied “norm” of the punishment in a stable way: after being punished following relatively higher contributions, they subsequently both reduce and later increase their contributions in different phases. This indicates a mixed or weakly shaped response: they do not engage in vendettas, and they also do not straightforwardly submit to the signal and permanently shift to lower or higher cooperation. They appear to treat punishment as part of the noisy environment rather than as a guiding signal for behavior. There is no strong evidence of targeted forgiveness or reconciliation either; their future actions toward those who likely punished them are indistinguishable from their generic behavior toward the group as a whole. Overall, they are non-retaliatory but also not clearly norm-following in response to being punished.

text: They are punished several times, including by players they themselves have sanctioned earlier. There is no observable retaliation against specific punishers: when they are punished, their subsequent behavior toward the likely punishers does not show systematic targeted counter-punishment. They also do not lower their contribution in a sustained way after being punished; any single low-contribution outlier does not persist. This indicates a relatively forgiving or non-reactive stance: they treat incoming punishment as part of the environment rather than as a personal affront requiring revenge. At the same time, their decision to stop using punishment over time might partially reflect recognizing that punishing others triggers counter-sanctions or antisocial dynamics, and thus is not worth it. Overall, they are not vindictive; they maintain their own cooperation standard and mostly disengage from sanctioning rather than escalate.

text: The player is punished in multiple rounds, sometimes heavily. When they are punished, they do not reliably respond with higher contributions or with reduced antisocial behavior. After being punished, they often continue to free-ride in later rounds and frequently continue to punish others. There is no clear evidence of compliance with punishers’ apparent intentions. There is also no clear pattern of symmetric retaliation specifically against those who punished them; because the transcript identifies all punishers and the player nonetheless punishes some of them on some occasions and not on others, we cannot cleanly separate normative punishment from revenge. However, since they continue to punish players they have already targeted earlier and maintain punishment even when they themselves are under attack, it is plausible that retaliation is one driver, but this is low-confidence. They do not appear to forgive in the sense of shifting toward more cooperative, norm-following behavior after being sanctioned. Instead, their reaction is to continue their own mixed strategy—free-riding frequently, occasionally contributing, and using punishment heavily. They do not show overt attempts to build peace through ceasing punishment after being hit themselves.

text: When targeted with punishment, this player does not escalate conflict. Even after being heavily punished in cooperative or neutral positions, they do not respond by punishing their likely punishers, despite punishers being identifiable. Instead, their main behavioral adjustment is on the contribution and reward margins: they reduce costly cooperation after harsh treatment and shift toward more self-protective, lower contributions and more selective prosocial acts. They show a form of “quiet withdrawal” rather than direct retaliation: they continue to participate and earn, but they decrease vulnerability. They do not display overt appeasement either; they do not clearly increase contributions to conform with enforcers’ apparent expectations. This pattern fits a personality that is conflict-avoidant in direct sanctioning, but responsive in the sense of adjusting exploitative/cooperative stance based on how fairly they feel treated.

cluster_intro: This persona cluster highlights individuals who exhibit resilience in the face of punishment, choosing not to retaliate against identifiable punishers. text: When they are punished, they do not change their contribution behavior, their use of punishment, or their use of rewards; they simply continue as before. There is no observable retaliation toward punishers, nor any apparent attempt to discipline them in later periods. Likewise, they do not appear to respond with appeasement: they do not reduce contributions to match others, nor do they start rewarding the individuals who punished them. Overall, their reaction to being punished is behaviorally inert. They neither escalate conflicts nor attempt to shape punishers’ incentives. This suggests that they either downplay the informational content of punishment, regard it as noise they should ignore, or follow a precommitted plan that does not incorporate feedback from sanctions. Emotional traits such as resentment or forgiveness are unobservable in this setting; behaviorally, they default to non-retaliation and continued cooperation.

text: They are punished multiple times by others, sometimes quite heavily relative to their own contribution and payoff. Following punishment, they do not show clear retaliatory behavior: they do not systematically punish back the specific individuals who target them, despite punishers being identifiable. Nor do they move toward mutual vendettas or expand their punishment usage after being hit. Their contribution response to being punished is mixed and not clearly compliant. In some instances they continue with low-to-mid contributions after punishment; in other instances they later raise contributions, but these changes coincide with shifts in the overall contribution climate, so the motive is ambiguous. There is no clear, tight pattern of “I was punished, so I start cooperating more toward that punisher or toward the group.” Overall, they behave as if punishment directed at them is mostly tolerated rather than avenged. They neither escalate aggression nor dramatically change their strategy in a clear, one-step way. This suggests a relatively cool, non-retaliatory temperament and possibly a belief that trying to fight back via punishment is not worth the cost. Their stance toward punishers is thus “non-escalating and weakly responsive”: they absorb the hit, carry on with their mixed cooperative strategy, and reserve their own sanctions for under-contributors rather than for personal retribution.

text: This player is punished multiple times by an identifiable peer but never responds with counter-punishment and does not adopt a clear appeasement pattern. After being targeted, they sometimes continue to free-ride and sometimes increase contributions; these shifts are inconsistent and do not align reliably with either escalation or compliance. The absence of any retaliatory punishment, even when they could cheaply reduce the punisher’s payoff, suggests either low concern for status or reputation vis-à-vis that individual, low desire for revenge, or a strategic choice that conflict is not worth the expense. Because their subsequent contribution behavior is mixed—sometimes moving to high contribution, sometimes back to zero—it is unclear whether they interpret punishment as a normative signal to cooperate; any compliance motive is at best weak and unreliable. Their stance toward punishers is best characterized as passive and non-escalatory: they absorb punishment without striking back and without a stable pattern of behavioral adjustment, implying a tendency to accept losses rather than engage in costly interpersonal contests.

text: When other players punish, especially when they direct costs toward high contributors or punish in ways that seem arbitrary or hostile, this player becomes confrontational rather than conciliatory. They retaliate by heavily punishing the punisher on later occasions, even when this is personally expensive and not clearly beneficial in terms of future payoffs. There is no clear evidence that they reduce contributions in direct response to being punished (their own contribution levels are already often low), nor that they increase contributions to appease punishers or align with their expectations. Compliance with external punishment pressure is therefore low; the dominant response is targeted retaliation, signaling a personality oriented toward defending their own perceived autonomy and status against others’ attempts at control.

text: When they defect from the contribution norm or send very low amounts, they are punished heavily by multiple others. They never respond with counter-punishment, even though punishers are identifiable. They also do not single out any punisher in chat for blame or confrontation. Instead, their reaction is behavioral adaptation: after being harshly sanctioned, they shift from opportunistic deviations toward stable high contributions. Their brief statement about having “found how to play” suggests they interpret punishment primarily as informational feedback about the payoff structure, not as a personal slight. They comply rather than resist. They show no signs of vendetta or grudges: they continue to cooperate alongside the same players who previously punished them without attempting to harm them later. This pattern is consistent with a pragmatic learner who accepts punishment as a signal and then moves on, rather than someone driven by anger or a need to restore personal dignity.

text: When this player is punished, they do not respond with simple withdrawal from cooperation. After receiving strong sanctions following a very low contribution, they increase their subsequent contributions back to moderate or high levels, indicating a degree of compliance with the cooperative signal. They do not appear to launch immediate, across-the-board retaliation via punishment right after being sanctioned; their punishment behavior before and after being punished remains focused on low contributors, not obviously on their own punishers. This points toward a relatively norm-responsive, not purely vindictive, style. At the same time, punishment they receive does not permanently pacify them. Later in the game, after being punished again, they continue to punish others in subsequent rounds, sometimes heavily. This suggests they are not intimidated into passivity by being a punishment target; instead, they maintain their role as an enforcer of contribution norms. There is no clear sign that they specifically counter-punish those who punished them (identities are not given), so whether they retaliate directly is unknown. The observable pattern fits a persona that accepts punishment feedback as part of the game, adjusts their own contribution behavior somewhat in response, but continues to hold others to account without clear personal grudges.

text: When they are punished despite fully cooperating, they do not change their behavior in any observable way. They maintain full contributions and continue to abstain from punishment. This indicates a highly forgiving or at least non-reactive stance. They do not appear to interpret punishment as a meaningful signal requiring adjustment; instead, they continue to follow their prior cooperative rule. They tolerate being treated unfairly without deviating from their pattern. Because punishers are identifiable to the punished in this environment, their failure to punish back suggests they either place low value on retribution, prioritize their own cooperative norm over reacting to perceived injustice, or simply disregard punishment as a relevant tool. They do not comply with or adapt to punishment in any strategic sense, because their baseline behavior (full cooperation, no punishment) already remains unchanged. Their type can therefore be characterized as steadfast, non-retaliatory, and resistant to social pressure conveyed through punishment.

cluster_title: Resilient Cooperation in the Face of Punishment
cluster_intro: Individuals in this group exhibit a remarkable resilience to punishment, maintaining their cooperative behavior regardless of negative sanctions. They do not retaliate or adjust their contributions after being punished, suggesting a strong commitment to group norms and collaboration.

text: When this player is punished, their immediate behavioral response is mixed but structured. After being punished following very low contributions, they do not escalate defection; instead, they soon return to relatively high contributions. This indicates that they read punishment as a signal of norm expectations and, at least partially, comply with it. They show capacity for behavioral adjustment in the cooperative direction after sanctions. At the same time, they also exhibit episodes of apparent defiance. After severe punishment for free-riding, they later again choose zero contributions in otherwise cooperative contexts, despite having learned that such behavior draws heavy sanctions. This suggests that punishment does not fully “tame” them; they sometimes reassert autonomy or test the group’s willingness to continue punishing. Their subsequent high contributions show that these defiant episodes are temporary rather than a permanent shift to antisocial behavior. They do not visibly reduce their punishment usage after being punished themselves; instead, they continue to deploy punishment in later rounds, occasionally at high levels. This implies that they do not interpret being punished as a reason to cease norm enforcement. There is no clear sign of direct counter-punishment (targeting those who punished them), because the identity of their targets is unknown; whether they retaliate against specific punishers versus only sanction low contributors is therefore unknown. Overall, their response to punishment balances compliance with persistence in their own enforcement role. They accept the existence of punishment as part of the game’s social contract, adjust contributions upward when sanctioning indicates group disapproval, but also periodically push back through renewed free-riding and sustained punishing of others. This combination is consistent with a strong-willed, norm-focused player who can be influenced by sanctions but is not fully deterred from occasionally challenging group norms.

text: When punished, this player does not exhibit submissive or purely compliant behavior. They do not appear to stop punishing or become strictly more cooperative in a sustained way after being sanctioned. Instead, they often continue or even increase their own punishment expenditures in nearby periods, which is consistent with retaliatory tendencies in a setting where punishers are identifiable. Their contributions after being punished do not reliably move in one direction: sometimes they raise contributions back to cooperative levels, at other times they maintain or reduce them. This mixed adjustment suggests that punishment alone does not straightforwardly discipline them; they interpret sanctions through the lens of ongoing social conflict rather than as clear signals to conform. They also do not show strong forgiveness in the sense of ceasing punishment after being targeted. Given that they keep using punishment mechanisms in rounds following sanctions against them, it is plausible that they either punish those they believe targeted them or at least continue norm enforcement without backing down. There is no evidence of them entering a prolonged punitive spiral that drives their own contributions to the minimum, but they accept repeated mutual punishment episodes. Overall, their response to punishers is assertive and somewhat resistant: they are not easily cowed by sanctions and may respond with further punishment rather than passive adjustment. Whether this is directed specifically at identified punishers or at broader low-contributors is unknown, but their pattern aligns with a player who values reciprocity and is willing to defend their stance even at a cost.

cluster_title: Resistance to Punishment in Cooperative Settings
cluster_intro: This persona exhibits a complex response to repeated punishment, often maintaining a mix of cooperative and uncooperative behaviors. Despite facing sanctions, they do not consistently comply with social norms or increase contributions, indicating a low sensitivity to punitive measures.

text: The player is punished once early on without an obvious preceding deviation from high cooperation. Their subsequent behavior does not include visible retaliation: they do not immediately start punishing others, do not reduce their contributions in response, and do not shift into a generally hostile pattern. Instead, they maintain very high contributions and abstain from punishment for a long stretch afterward. This indicates a forgiving or at least non-escalatory stance toward being targeted. They do not clearly “comply” with a new norm as they were already cooperating at a high level, so there is no observable behavioral adjustment toward the punisher’s perceived expectations. Overall, when punished and when punishment sources are identifiable, they respond with continued cooperation and almost no punitive response, suggesting a preference for stability and norm adherence over reciprocal aggression.

cluster_intro: This persona cluster exhibits a consistent pattern of non-retaliation in response to punishment. Players maintain high levels of cooperation and do not adjust their contributions or engage in punitive behaviors against those who penalize them. Their behavior suggests a strong commitment to cooperative strategies, regardless of external sanctions.

text: This player is heavily punished multiple times and never responds with obvious counter-punishment against any specific low-contributing peer, despite punishers being identifiable in principle. They also never escalate their own punishment usage after suffering large deductions, which suggests they do not pursue retaliation as a primary motive. Instead, the main adjustment they make to heavy punishment is behavioral: after suffering especially severe deductions, they temporarily increase their contribution level, at times contributing fully. This pattern is consistent with at least partial compliance or appeasement rather than revenge. Their compliance is not durable; after a high-contribution response, they revert to low or token contributions, indicating that punishment moves them in the short run but does not fully reset their internal strategy. They may interpret punishment as an immediate threat to be defused rather than a reliable, sustained social norm that must be followed. There is no clear sign of “teaching” behavior toward punishers (for example, punishing them back), nor of forgiveness as such—rather, they seem to pragmatically absorb punishment and adjust contributions transiently while leaving their own punishment activity largely unchanged. They thus present as a non-retaliatory, short-horizon responder to sanctions: they accept that punishment matters enough to sometimes increase cooperation, but they neither repay punishers nor adopt a long-term cooperative commitment afterward.

text: When punished, this player does not consistently retaliate; instead, they react in a mixed but generally non‑escalatory way. After being punished in some rounds, they increase or maintain relatively high contributions afterward rather than dropping to minimal levels or launching obvious counter‑punishment bursts. This indicates that they are at least partially compliant with the signaling implied by punishment: they treat punishment as information about what others may expect, and they sometimes adjust their behavior in a more cooperative direction following it. At the same time, they occasionally continue to punish others after having been punished themselves, which suggests they do not simply internalize punishment as a reason to stop enforcing norms. They appear able to separate “I was punished” from “there are still low contributors who should be sanctioned.” There is no clear evidence that they identify and target their own punishers for revenge; since punishers are identifiable in principle but we do not see explicit matching behavior, their retaliation tendency remains unknown or low‑confidence. What can be inferred is that they do not show a clear pattern of tit‑for‑tat punishment spirals. Overall, their reaction to being punished combines a degree of acceptance and persistence. Instead, they continue to pursue their intermediate cooperative standard and to use punishment as a tool for norm enforcement, even when they themselves receive deductions. This suggests a personality that is relatively resilient, norm‑driven, and not easily pushed into extremes of spite or submission by others’ punitive actions.

text: When punished, they do not respond with observable aggression, counter-punishment, or a lasting shift to defection. After experiencing strong sanctions for free-riding or low contributions, they quickly revert to high contributions and maintain them for some time. This pattern is consistent with behavioral “compliance” to the group’s implicit high-cooperation standard rather than open resistance. They also show a degree of resilience: being punished does not cause a permanent breakdown in cooperation. They neither stubbornly continue to free-ride nor launch a spiral of mutual punishment. Instead, they move back toward the cooperative norm. Their later low contributions, following earlier punishments, look more like exploration and strategic testing than revenge; they are not directed specifically at the known punishers (no targeted punishment; contributions are anonymous across targets), and the player still often returns to full contribution afterward. There is no sign of direct retaliation against any identifiable punisher via the punishment mechanism. They do not use their available power to “hit back,” even when they themselves are clearly on the receiving end of substantial deductions. This suggests a relatively forgiving or at least non-retaliatory disposition: they absorb sanctions and adjust their own behavior but do not seek retribution. At the same time, punishment does not produce perfect long-run obedience: after complying with high contributions for a while, they again test the boundaries with lower contributions later. This is consistent with them respecting the general cooperative norm while periodically re-evaluating how strictly it is enforced and what they can get away with. Overall, their style is: accept sanctions without counter-attacking, temporarily comply more fully, then cautiously probe again, all without ever using direct punishment themselves.

text: When they themselves are punished, their behavior shows no sign of retaliation, withdrawal, or strategic accommodation. They continue to contribute fully and do not reduce their giving in response to being targeted. They also do not clearly escalate punishment afterward in a way that can be unambiguously interpreted as revenge against specific punishers; their subsequent punishment remains aligned with under-contribution rather than with who has harmed them. This suggests that they either discount the personal slight of being punished, interpret it as part of the enforcement environment, or simply maintain their own norm regardless of others’ actions. They show no visible attempt to “teach a lesson” specifically to their own punisher beyond what they already do to low contributors in general. Compliance, in the sense of changing contributions in response to sanctions, is absent because they are already at maximum cooperation and never deviate. Their style toward punishers is best characterized as steady and impersonal: they keep enforcing contribution norms and keep fully cooperating, without observable grudges or appeasement.

cluster_title: Resilient Cooperation in the Face of Punishment
cluster_intro: Individuals in this group exhibit a remarkable resilience to punishment, maintaining their cooperative behavior regardless of negative sanctions. They do not retaliate or adjust their contributions after being punished, suggesting a strong commitment to group norms and collaboration.

text: This player does not collapse into defection when punished. After receiving nontrivial deductions, they quickly return to high contributions and, in some cases, maintain or even increase their willingness to punish others’ under-contribution. This suggests resilience and a relatively stable commitment to their own cooperation norm rather than simple conformity under threat. Their reaction to being punished is not clearly compliant in the sense of adopting others’ presumed expectations; instead, they persist in viewing high contribution as appropriate and continue to expect it from others. There is no clear evidence that they deliberately retaliate against specific punishers, because the transcript does not reveal who punished whom (unknown). It is possible they interpret punishment against themselves as misguided or unfair yet do not respond by targeting high contributors; their continued focus on under-contributors as the plausible punishment targets suggests they remain norm-focused rather than shifting into vendetta mode (moderate but not certain). They also do not appear to adopt a long-run defection strategy in response to being punished. Instances of lower contribution after heavy punishment are temporary and partial, not a full switch to persistent free-riding. Over time, their behavior shows a pattern of forgiveness or at least pragmatic continuation: despite prior sanctions against them, they re-engage in cooperative, high-contribution play and keep trying to shape the group through selective punishment of low contributors. This portrays a personality that is firm on principles, relatively forgiving in action, and not easily pushed into spiteful non-cooperation by others’ sanctions.

text: This player is highly sensitive to being punished, but their response is complex and not simply compliant. When they are heavily sanctioned after defecting, they do not double down immediately on free-riding; instead, they show a capacity to escalate their contributions substantially in subsequent periods. This indicates that punishment does convey information and pressure they take seriously. They appear to understand that the group is willing and able to impose large costs on them for deviating and sometimes react by raising contributions toward or up to full cooperation. However, this compliance tends to be unstable. After a run of high contributions and relative safety from sanctions, they drift back into lower contributions or free-riding, even though the earlier punishments were quite severe. This “short-lived compliance” suggests that punishment updates their behavior in the short term but does not fundamentally realign their preferences or erase their willingness to exploit when they perceive an opening. In effect, they treat punishment as a signal that they need to adjust for a while, but not as a permanent constraint. They do not respond to punishment with immediate, direct, and sustained forgiveness. The fact that they later use the punishment mechanism themselves in environments where they have previously suffered sanctions is consistent with some degree of resentment or desire to rebalance perceived injustices. At the same time, they do not engage in an all-out vendetta: they do not engage in punishment every period after being punished and do not permanently withhold cooperation. This indicates partial forgiveness and a willingness to re-enter cooperation, but without fully abandoning the urge to retaliate or protect their dignity. They seem to infer that punishers are capable of coordinating to inflict large deductions and that sanctions can recur if they defect. Their strategy thus becomes cyclical: periods of high cooperation when the threat of punishment feels salient, followed by more self-serving contributions once they feel the pressure has eased or that they have “earned” some slack. They do not escalate defection purely out of spite, which suggests their response incorporates both emotional and strategic elements. Their social style toward punishers can be summarized as grudgingly responsive: they acknowledge the power of the punishment system enough to adjust behavior, but they also retain a readiness to use punishment themselves and to resume opportunism when they believe they can get away with it.

cluster_title: Resistance to Punishment in Cooperative Settings
cluster_intro: This persona exhibits a complex response to repeated punishment, often maintaining a mix of cooperative and uncooperative behaviors. Despite facing sanctions, they do not consistently comply with social norms or increase contributions, indicating a low sensitivity to punitive measures.

text: This player is highly sensitive to being punished and tends to respond more with defiance or mixed retaliation than with simple compliance. When they receive relatively mild punishment after contributing slightly below a fully cooperative level, they do not reliably increase contributions to align with the strictest norm; instead, they often either maintain moderate contributions or later decrease them. As punishment intensifies against them, they progressively lower their contributions, sometimes to very low levels despite others continuing to contribute generously. This pattern suggests they interpret punishment not purely as informative feedback about expected behavior, but also as hostile or unfair treatment that justifies pulling back cooperation. They do not exhibit a stable “forgive and comply” pattern. After being punished, they sometimes still contribute generously in subsequent interactions when the environment looks highly cooperative and when they may feel less directly targeted. However, as they are punished more severely, their later decisions reflect durable distrust: they reduce contributions and at times continue to spend on punishment themselves. This indicates a mixture of resentment and willingness to strike back at perceived aggressors or at least at low contributors, even if doing so is costly. Because punishers are identifiable, it is likely (though not certain) that they sometimes direct punishment toward those who punished them, especially when those same players also under-contribute relative to others (low confidence, since the specific mapping is not shown). Their long-run trajectory—high cooperation and strong norm-enforcing punishment devolving into lower contributions and ongoing punishment amid mounting deductions—indicates that once their relationship with the group becomes conflictual, they do not simply adapt by quietly conforming. Instead, they partially withdraw cooperation and maintain or repurpose punishment as an expression of resistance or retribution rather than as a purely cooperative enforcement tool. In interpersonal terms, they appear proud and reciprocity-focused: they reward fair treatment with high cooperation and constructive norm enforcement, but react poorly to perceived unfair sanctions, becoming less cooperative and more willing to engage in punitive conflict rather than acquiescing to others’ demands.

text: When this player is punished, they do not escalate hostility and do not permanently collapse their cooperative behavior. After being punished once while still having contributed fully, they continue to contribute fully in subsequent interactions and do not start a broad retaliation campaign. This indicates that they tolerate some level of negative treatment without abandoning the public good. They also show at least one mild retaliatory or expressive response: after being punished while contributing fully, they later withhold contribution completely in a round where others largely continue to cooperate. This is temporally consistent with a “you punished me, I will show I can also free-ride” reaction, though the exact target of their feelings is unknown. They do not accompany this with punishment that round, and afterward they quickly return to their previous high-contribution pattern. This suggests that any retaliation motive is limited and short-lived rather than defining. When they are punished again near the end while behaving cooperatively, they answer with a combination of continued full contribution and a small amount of punishment. This looks like a calibrated response: they do not abandon cooperation, but they do signal disapproval via limited sanctioning. They appear more focused on reasserting norms than on vendettas. Overall, in response to punishment they are resilient and norm-oriented: they mostly keep cooperating, occasionally express displeasure via a brief defection or small counter-punishment, but do not spiral into sustained conflict. Their default after negative treatment is to return to high cooperation rather than to disengage or aggressively retaliate.

text: When they themselves are punished, this player neither collapses into persistent low contribution nor shifts into indiscriminate retaliatory punishment. Their first notable punishment received coincides with them contributing less than the rest, followed by a later return to high contributions. This is consistent with partial compliance: they interpret punishment as a norm signal and adjust back toward the group standard rather than escalating conflict. They do not target high contributors after being punished, which argues against a vengeful or spiteful response pattern. Instead, they continue to use punishment selectively against under-contributors. This behavior points to a relatively “forgiving but principled” style: they absorb punishment without turning it into a vendetta, but they maintain their own enforcement standards toward others. However, their contributions do show some volatility after being punished; they occasionally contribute less than their previous high baseline in later periods. This may reflect a mixture of compliance and guardedness: they acknowledge the signal to contribute more, but the experience of being sanctioned when others’ behavior is unstable may also lower their willingness to unconditionally cooperate in every subsequent round. They do not appear to directly reward punishers or to display special deference to them beyond raising their contribution back toward the cooperative norm. In summary, when punished they tend to (a) avoid retaliating directly against likely punishers, (b) partially restore norm-consistent behavior, and (c) preserve their identity as a cooperative norm enforcer rather than becoming submissive or vindictive.
